metadata.summary: Sources grounded across 8 research areas: visual perception science, information visualization theory, chart-type selection, color theory for monitoring, dashboard layout, time-series visualization, observability-specific patterns, anti-patterns.

sources.title: Graphical Perception: Theory, Experimentation, and Application to the Development of Graphical Methods
sources.publication: Journal of the American Statistical Association
sources.key_findings: Established the ordering of elementary perceptual tasks by accuracy: position along common scale > positions along non-aligned scales > length, direction, angle > area > volume, curvature > shading, color saturation.

sources.title: Elementary Perceptual Tasks
sources.key_findings: Confirms Cleveland-McGill ordering with task descriptions and chart-type mappings.

sources.title: Alberto Cairo's hierarchy of elementary perceptual tasks
sources.key_findings: Modernized version of Cleveland-McGill, with practical chart-selection rule: 'use tasks as high in the hierarchy as possible.'

sources.title: Harnessing Preattentive Processes for Multivariate Data Visualization
sources.key_findings: Identifies pre-attentive features as colour, orientation, intensity, size, shape, curvature, line length. Hue and orientation operate independently — combinable for multivariate encoding.

sources.title: Data-Ink Ratio
sources.key_findings: Definition: proportion of ink used to represent data vs total ink. Tufte's principle 'above all else show the data.' Includes Inbar et al. (2007) counter-finding that some 'non-data ink' aids interpretation.

sources.title: Tufte chartjunk lie factor data-ink small multiples sparklines (Perplexity research synthesis)
sources.key_findings: Lie Factor = size-of-effect-in-graphic / size-of-effect-in-data; ~1.0 is honest, far from 1 is distortion. Sparklines = tiny axis-less line charts for intra-period trends. Small multiples = repeated identical chart format across categories/time for direct comparison. Data density = info per visual unit; high density preferred.

sources.title: Dashboards Explained: Operational, Strategic and Analytical
sources.key_findings: Stephen Few's three dashboard types: Strategic (high-level KPIs, org-wide), Analytical (drill-down, departmental, exploratory), Operational (real-time monitoring, glanceable).

sources.title: Information Dashboard Design: Displaying Data for At-A-Glance Monitoring
sources.key_findings: Three pillars: Clarity (no decoration), Purpose-driven design, At-a-glance monitoring as the goal of operational dashboards.

sources.title: ColorBrewer 2.0
sources.key_findings: Three palette types: Sequential (ordered low-to-high), Diverging (meaningful midpoint), Qualitative (categorical). Colorblind-safe filter available. 3-12 classes supported.

sources.title: RColorBrewer Package Documentation
sources.key_findings: Sequential = lightness steps low-to-high. Diverging = equal emphasis on midpoint and extremes; light colors in middle, dark at both ends. Qualitative = no inherent ordering.

sources.title: Coloring for Colorblindness
sources.key_findings: Interactive simulator for testing palettes under deuteranopia/protanopia/tritanopia. Foundational reference for colorblind safety.

sources.title: Designing for Color blindness
sources.key_findings: Deuteranopia most common. 8-color Wong palette (Nature Methods Points of View) is the foundational categorical safe palette. Use perceptual luminance uniformity in color-blind space; LCH luminance steps.

sources.title: Color blindness (Points of View)
sources.key_findings: 8-color palette designed for colorblind safety, widely adopted as 'Okabe-Ito' or 'Wong' palette. Hex codes: #000000 #E69F00 #56B4E9 #009E73 #F0E442 #0072B2 #D55E00 #CC79A7.

sources.title: 5 Tips on Designing Colorblind-Friendly Visualizations
sources.key_findings: Don't use red/green together; substitute with blue/red, blue/orange. About 8% of males, 0.5% of females have red-green color vision deficiency.

sources.title: The RED Method: How to Instrument Your Services
sources.key_findings: RED = Rate (req/s), Errors (failing req), Duration (latency). Complement of USE; RED for services/microservices, USE for hardware. Mirrors Google's Four Golden Signals (RED + Saturation).

sources.title: Common Observability Strategies
sources.key_findings: RED for user-facing services; USE for hardware (CPU/memory/network); Four Golden Signals = RED + Saturation. Alert on RED (symptoms), use USE to identify causes.

sources.title: Site Reliability Engineering — Monitoring Distributed Systems
sources.key_findings: Four Golden Signals = Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation. Standard SRE monitoring framework.

sources.title: The USE Method
sources.key_findings: USE = Utilization, Saturation, Errors. Applied to every resource (CPU, memory, network device). Complement to RED for hardware monitoring.

sources.title: Defining Burn Rates
sources.key_findings: Burn rate = error rate / error budget. Burn rate = 1 means on pace to exhaust budget. Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts use short + long windows together to balance noise and recovery.

sources.title: SLO Calculator — Burn Rate Alert Visualizer
sources.key_findings: Standard 14.4x burn rate triggers when 2% of budget is consumed in 1 hour for a 30-day SLO. Formula: burn_rate = (budget%/100) * (window_days * 24 / alert_hours).

sources.title: Aspect Ratio and Banking to 45 Degrees
sources.key_findings: Banking to 45° = aspect ratio that makes average line slope ≈ 45°. Cleveland-McGill-McGill 1988. Specifically for slope comparison between lines, not universal. Talbot et al. (2011) found shallower slopes can outperform; treat as heuristic not rule.

sources.title: Multi-Scale Banking to 45°
sources.key_findings: Mathematical analysis of banking. Average-absolute-orientation banking. Multi-scale version handles charts with varying line scales.

sources.title: Line chart aspect ratios
sources.key_findings: Wide aspect ratio flattens trends; tall aspect ratio dramatises them. Banking to 45° is a reasonable starting heuristic.

sources.title: Why Two Y-Axes (Y2Y): A Case Study for Visual Correlation
sources.key_findings: Dual-axis useful for visual correlation when magnitude matters and normalization would obscure context. Acceptable for export/print/seminar use; problematic when scales can be arbitrarily aligned to manufacture correlation.

sources.title: Seven Anti-Patterns for Analytics Dashboards
sources.key_findings: Pie chart anti-pattern, 3D effects anti-pattern; reinforces Cleveland-McGill perceptual hierarchy as the WHY behind each.

sources.title: Common Mistakes in Data Visualization
sources.key_findings: Comprehensive catalog: truncated axes (violate lie-factor), dual axes (manufacture correlation), 3D charts (distort perception), rainbow colormaps (jet is not perceptually uniform, colorblind-unsafe), >7 pie slices (poor angle judgment), chartjunk (steals attention from data).

sources.title: Visualization Analysis and Design
sources.key_findings: Three-question framework: What (data abstraction), Why (task abstraction), How (visual encoding via marks & channels). Channel effectiveness: magnitude (ordered) channels rank position > length > tilt/angle > area > depth > color luminance > color saturation > curvature > volume. Identity (categorical) channels rank: spatial region > color hue > motion > shape.

sources.title: F-Shaped Pattern of Reading on the Web: Misunderstood, But Still Relevant
sources.key_findings: F-pattern appears when text is unformatted, user prioritizes efficiency, low engagement. Mitigation: clear headings, bullets, visual hierarchy. Multiple scanning patterns exist (layer-cake, spotted, marking, bypassing, commitment).

sources.title: Dashboard Layout: Visual Hierarchy & Structure
sources.key_findings: Z-pattern for sparse (KPI cards), F-pattern for dense (operational). Top-left = first eye landing. Inverted pyramid: Layer 1 = status/targets, Layer 2 = trends/context, Layer 3 = detail/drill-down. Five-second glance test.

sources.title: The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two
sources.key_findings: Working memory limits ≈ 7±2 chunks. Implication for dashboards: keep top-level groups within 5-9 items before requiring sub-grouping/drill-down.

sources.title: 7 Gestalt principles for data visualization
sources.key_findings: Proximity, similarity, enclosure, closure, continuity, connection, symmetry — with dashboard applications.

sources.title: Visual Hierarchy and Gestalt Psychology — Data Visualization Tutorial
sources.key_findings: Common region / closure: enclosing data points in a rectangle makes them appear related. Connection: lines between points are stronger grouping than proximity.

sources.title: Visual communication — Core design principles for displaying quantitative information
sources.key_findings: Principle: use length or 2-D location of objects to encode quantitative values unless those channels are already used. Pre-attentive attributes are processed quickly without conscious effort.

research_gaps.gap: Cleveland-McGill original PDF could not be parsed (binary)
research_gaps.mitigation: Two independent secondary sources converged on the same ordering; ranking is well-established and uncontroversial.

research_gaps.gap: Munzner channel rankings could not be parsed from primary PDF
research_gaps.mitigation: Search snippets and Vienna slide summary aligned on the framework; ranking is well-documented in the field.

research_gaps.gap: Coloring for Colorblindness page rendered empty
research_gaps.mitigation: Krzywinski palettes, Wong/Okabe-Ito reference, Tableau guidance, and metricgate all cover colorblind-safe design.

research_gaps.gap: Few's book contents only summarized, not directly accessed
research_gaps.mitigation: Chartio summary plus secondary explainers cover the three-dashboard-type typology adequately for skill-level rules.
